speaker: interviewee
text: [AUDIO TEST TONE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And we're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is January 31, 1996. And we are interviewing Mrs. Agnes Vertes, maiden name Katz. My name is Michele Landa-Brooker. We're conducting this interview in Weston, Connecticut. And the interview language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is January 31, 1996. My name is Michele Landa-Brooker. And we are interviewing Mrs. Agnes Vertes, maiden name Katz. We are conducting this interview in English and in Weston, Connecticut.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you start by telling me your full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Agnes Margaret Vertes. I was born Agnes Margaret Katz in Budapest, Hungary, September 4, 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me something about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I come from a religious Jewish family, Orthodox. On one side of my family, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on my mother's side of the family, they were not Hasidim, but very religious. And my grandfather had a beard. And my grandmother wore a sheitel. And generally, that section of my family was very, very observant. My father's family, the Katzes, were also observant Jews, but they were more modern. They wore modern dress. And they wore-- they were educated. Not to say that the Gluck family, my mother's family, were not educated, but they were educated in Hebrew, mostly in yeshivas. And my father's family were doctors and lawyers, et cetera. More modern, taking advantage of the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] equality that was the-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] that-- that was available to the Jews in the 1900s in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me your mother and father's complete names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was born Irene Gluck, 'Glook' in Hungarian. And my father was born Armin Katz. And my mother was born March 4, 1914, in Nyirbator. You don't want me to spell that, do you? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: If you can. If not, we'll do it later.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's spelled N-Y-I-R-B-A-- with an accent on it-- --T-O-R. And that's in the plains of Hungary. It's a community of, I don't know, maybe 10,000 people. And it had a-- a fairly sizable Jewish community, compared to the-- as compared to the entire community. My father was born in Maramarossziget. And-- but he was brought up in Budapest. My grandparents, Katz, were first cousins. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Leopold Katz and Cecilia Katz were first cousins. And the story goes that my grandfather was marriageable age. And his uncle-- that's my great-grandfather on my grandmother's side-- was taking him around to look for a bride. And after they visited many households-- because that's the way it was done there. They hired a carriage, or whatever was fashionable at the time. I don't think they had automobiles yet. And they went around to the different Jewish households with eligible maidens. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And after my grandfather went to a number of them, he turned to his uncle and he said, you know what? I don't like any of these girls, but I love your daughter Cecilia, and I'd like to marry her. [LAUGHS] So that's just a little aside. That's how they got married.

speaker: interviewee
text: Once they got married, they moved up to Budapest. And the two-- my two great-grandfathers established them in business. And however, they keep-- kept going back and forth to Maramarossziget and Budapest. And when my father was born, my grandmother happened to have been in Maramarossziget.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, the family had lived since about 1910, I would say, continuously in Budapest. And the business itself was headed by my grandfather to begin with, but it grew and grew, and it was a very profitable business. And it was a textile export-import business. Also, they manufactured kerchiefs and stockings and things of that-- that nature. And they were importing from Italy and England, and they were exporting to I don't know what countries. I really don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, when my father became marriageable age, he-- he was at that time already a university graduate. And he went into the family business, because my grandfather got a stroke at an early age, and my grandmother was the head of the business. And she wanted him to become a medical doctor, but my father didn't really want to be a medical doctor. And besides, he didn't want to go out of the country to go to a university, because at that time, there was a law in effect called numerus clausus, which meant that Jewish people were only a certain very small percentage of Jewish people could attend a university. And depending on what connections they had, they could get into some, and they couldn't get into some others. And my uncle, for instance, my-- my father's brother, went to the University of Prague, because that's where he got accepted. In Hungary, he couldn't go. And my father didn't want to leave the country. And he went to the University of Economics, or-- I don't know how to translate it, because it's not an exact translation of a business university here. Anyway, so he went into business with my grandmother. And he was very, very successful. And when it came time to get married, he met my mother, who was a very beautiful woman. But she was already an old maid. She was, I guess, 24 when they met. [LAUGHS] And you know, in the 1930s, that was old. [LAUGHS] And my mother was not as educated as my father. And as I mentioned earlier, she came from a very religious family. And my grandfather on my mother's side was a grain merchant in Nyirbator, but he went bankrupt because his partner embezzled a great deal of money. And his partner had, I think, six or seven kids. And my grandfather had to sell everything to bail out his partner. Because otherwise, his partner would have gone to jail. So they became very, very poor. And they moved up to Budapest from Nyirbator. They had to sell their home and whatever they owned. And up in Budapest, he became a mashgiach, which is, to explain it to those who don't know what it is, it's a person whose responsibility is to check in places where they prepare food that the foods are prepared according to the laws of kashrut, meaning that the food is kosher. So that's what my grandfather did. And that did not suffice for all the children and for a big household. So they were rather poor. My mother had no dowry. And I only know my mother's side of the story, so this may or may not be exactly what happened. But when my grandmother on my father's side found out that my mother-- my mother was going to be my father's bride, she was very upset, because my father was a very eligible bachelor, and they were hoping to make a really good match for him so that maybe two family businesses will become even more successful than they were. And-- however, my father was so much in love with my mother that he didn't budge. And the marriage, of course, took place. They got married August 20, 1939, just before the war broke out. As a matter of fact, they were supposed to go on their honeymoon to Italy, and they had to cancel it. So my mother never got to go outside of the country, because, as I said, she wasn't a well-to-do girl, and she didn't travel. While my father-- [COUGHS] excuse me-- my father has traveled to Italy and to Austria, or wherever he went skiing, and also to bring in orders from England or Italy, wherever they were importing stuff from. Textiles, that is. So she got cheated out of that honeymoon. Once they got married, there was a tremendous apartment shortage in Hungary, so they moved in with my grandparents, Gluck. And they lived there till I was born in 1940. And I was still born in that place. And at that point, my father asked his mother for a raise. And she refused. So he went into business for himself. And he was so successful-- it was a shortage economy. And the big thing was to obtain the goods. And that's what he was very good at. And a few months later, he was making tremendous amounts of money. And Grandma was going bankrupt.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Grandma came begging him to go back. And he drove a hard bargain. He said that, yeah, I'll come back, but we need an apartment. We need furniture. And I want everything for my wife. So Grandma got us an apartment close to the business and furnished it fully and brought in from Maramarossziget-- because nothing else would do-- a Jewish woman as a cook, and also a woman to take care of me, a nanny. I think she was French, although I don't remember speaking any French. [LAUGHS] And so my mother had three servants, and so she did very well. And this was fine until the Nazis came into power in Hungary. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: As I said, we lived near the business, which was-- the business was at [? Loudon ?] Street. [? Loudon ?] Street 5. My grandparents lived in [? Loudon ?] Street 3, which is the next building. And we lived around the corner on [NON-ENGLISH] Street. And I remember the building. I-- I have very clear memories of it. And we-- we had-- I should say, I had a charmed life before the war. It was easy, at least for me as a child. I traveled with my parents wherever they went. And at home, I had-- I can remember I had a charge account. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you imagine? A little three-year-old I was, and I had a charge account in the fanciest chocolate store on the Andrassy Avenue, in-- which is a wide boulevard similar to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what's the name of it, the one in Paris? Champs-Elysees?

speaker: interviewee
text: Champs-Elysees, yeah. Things don't come to me sometimes. [LAUGHS] Anyway, of course, it wasn't as nice as the Champs-Elysees, but it was very nice. And I-- I still remember, I used to go to these people called [NON-ENGLISH], that was their name. And I picked out whatever I wanted, and my father would pay the bill monthly. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So as I said, life went on OK. And all the restrictions, all the Jewish laws did not affect me. Because when, for instance, Jews could no longer own businesses, my family had paid somebody called a straw man, they called him, who was a Christian who sold his or her name to Jews so that they could continue their businesses. And they got a small share of the profits, but business went on as usual.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in 1942, they started taking the Jewish men between the ages of 18 and 60, I believe. It had to be between the ages of 18 and 60 because my grandfathers, they were close to-- well, 50-- they were about 55, and they were still taken. So I'm assuming that it was up to 60. And they did take my Grandfather Gluck. They didn't take my Grandfather Katz because my Grandfather Katz was disabled. They took my father. And my father, however, was very lucky, in a sense. Because he decided that he was going to no camp. He was not going to any camp. And essentially, this was not a camp on the scale of Bergen-Belsen or any of those camps. These were units attached to the regular Hungarian Army which performed slave labor. They had to provide their own uniforms, and they had to wear a yellow armband and-- just to signify they were Jews-- and they got much smaller rations. And some of them were lucky and were treated well by their army officers, and some of them were not. Now, my father, as I said, he was on the train, being taken out of the country, and he just didn't want to leave. And while the train was passing through a wheat field, he jumped off, and he hid in the wheat field. And somehow under the cover of darkness, he made his way home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then my grandmother got into action. And my father had a schoolmate whose name was Dr. Lehodski. And he was a neurologist. So Grandma and my father went to him and said, can you give him a certificate that he's not normal? And they didn't take people to slave labor who weren't quite right up there. And so he gave my father the certificate.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the meantime, in 19-- the end of 1942-- because he was taken earlier in 1942, and then he escaped-- end of 1942, my sister was born. And sometime early in 1943, they came for him again. And at that point, my mother went to open the door so that the two officers are there.

speaker: interviewer
text: My father quickly got into the bed in the bedroom and put my sister next to him on the bed. And when the officers came in, he let my sister drop to the Oriental rug. I mean, he knew that she wasn't going to get hurt. But he let her drop. And my mother turned to these officers and said, you see how crazy he is? He throws down his own child.

speaker: interviewee
text: And these happen to have been army officers, and not the [NON-ENGLISH], who where were the Hungarian Nazis. And they very gallantly kissed my mother's hand. And they said, oh, we're very sorry for you, madam. And they left. So my father escaped again. [LAUGHS] And this went on and on. Every time there was something, and somehow he got out of it. And it was just luck. He could have been taken. These people could have said that, OK, get out of bed, and let's go. But it just so happened they didn't. And then things went on again. This is still 1943. Most of the men were not home anymore. And most people did not believe that anything terrible was going to happen. But by 1943, I remember that in our house, we always had people for dinner who were these people. They were Polish Jews who escaped from Poland. And they were telling horrible tales, which I, of course, didn't hear. But I remember that they were there. So my father must've known what was happening in Poland. And people just went on and didn't think that anything terrible was going to happen. Also, the Jewish congregation-- the heads of the Jewish congregation, they kept saying, with each law that was passed, OK, so it's not so bad. Let's give it to them. The Nazis want our cars, let's give it to them. And as long as we are here, we're home, we're living the way we used to. OK, so you're not an engineer anymore because you can't work as an engineer. You're not a university professor because you cannot work as a university professor. But you're still alive, and everything is OK. And this, too, shall pass. This is how everybody thought-- this, too, shall pass. And of course, also, there were big Hungarian-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] how should I say-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they really thought that they were through-and-through Hungarians, and nobody was going to throw them out of there. And they fought in the First World War. 10,000 Jews died in the First World War fighting on the side of the Hungarians, which was pretty stupid on their-- their side. But they thought that they were doing the right thing by their country, for their countrymen. And they were so eager to become Hungarian that they went, and they were really brave. And as I said, I know 10,000 of them died.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I don't know if much credence was given to the tales of these Polish people. To the-- well, they weren't tales. They were their experiences they were relating. But I'm pretty sure that my father knew about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And also, in 1942, already the bombing started. And I remember going down to the shelter in 1943. And I was terrified of bombs. Terrified. Absolutely terrified. I was a kid. I mean, I was a baby. I didn't know what it was, but it was something terrible. It was noisy. It created destruction. And it terrified me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, as I said, we had these Polish people come. But the heads of the congregation wanted to keep calm. Everybody keep calm, this, too, shall pass. So most people didn't think very much as to what they were going to do, until March 19 of 1944, when the Germans finally came into Hungary. And they just occupied the country [SNAPS] overnight.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at that point, every Jew had to start wearing a yellow star on the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] left-hand side, 10 centimeters in diameter, and-- except children under the age of six. So I never wore a star, because I was under the age of six. But my parents did. And I would like to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] dispel the notion that the Hungarians just were oppressed, and because the Germans were there, that was the only reason they persecuted Jews. The Hungarians were just as vicious, if not more vicious, than the Germans. Much more vicious. And to tell you how vicious they were, I'll give you two incidents, one in which I was present as a child, and I remember, and one where my-- my father was present. Or rather, my father's experience. My father was going on the streets. He was coming and going-- he was very brave-- without-- without the star. And If you wore the-- if you didn't wear the star, it was punishable by even death, depending on who your judge was, not that there was a court of law, but, I mean, dependent on who caught you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father was going somewhere in Budapest, and all of a sudden, somebody yells at him, oh, now I got you, Armin! And he happened to have been a disgruntled employee who was fired because he was stealing. So he saw my father, and he took my father to the nearest police station. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and he took them-- he-- he took him there to denounce him, turn him in. He didn't get anything out of it. This was just plain anti-Semitism. Plain viciousness.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so my father was again in a situation where he knew that he was going to be taken away on a train. And this wasn't going to be slave labor anymore. This was going to be what they called [NON-ENGLISH], which means they killed you. And he ended up in this huge room with a number of his coreligionists who were there for that purpose. And he thought, my god, I'm dead if I don't get out of here. And of course, there were guards. And he was carrying a briefcase. And he looked outside, and he saw there were some trucks outside in the courtyard.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when the-- one of the guards came over, he said to him, would you be kind enough to come with me to the bathroom? Because you couldn't go alone. So he asked him to escort him to the bathroom. And once they got there, he gave him the briefcase, somehow insinuating there was-- there was money in it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And once he went into the bathroom, he found a window, in which he got out. And he crawled under one of the trucks. And when the truck went outside of the courtyard, he went outside to the street. And then he went upstairs to somebody's house-- not somebody's house. Into one of the apartment buildings, because he knew that there was going to be a dragnet. Definitely, they were going to look for him. And he rang the first bell. And he was so lucky again. This was just luck. There was a-- a gentile woman, who happened to be the wife of a judge, who said, you stay here, and I dare them to come into my place. And indeed, a few minutes later, they came. They rang the bell.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was quite indignant when they asked her, is-- did a Jew come here to hide? And-- how dare! And they-- of course, before that, she told my father to hide in the closet. And my father heard all this. But they left. And so he escaped again.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the other that I wanted to tell you, just to show you that it wasn't just the Germans, it was the Hungarian Nazis, as well. When the bombings were very, very heavy in Budapest, I remember we went to Kiskoros, which is also on the plains, but further south than where my mother was born. We had relatives in Kiskoros. And we went there because the family thought that we would be safer there because they were not going to bomb a small village. The Allies are not interested in bombing the fields. So these relatives of ours had vineyards, and we went down there. And this was in March of 1944. In the meantime, the Germans came in. And we were down in Kiskoros, and my father was up in Budapest. And my father decided that we were safer in a city among a lot of people, among more than a million people, than in a small town. And so he came down to get us. This was maybe March 19 or 20. Right after the Germans came in. He wanted the family together. On the other hand, my sister and I-- this I remember-- I was very, very ill. So was my sister. We had measles, a very high fever. Now, at that time already, Jews were not able to travel. They weren't-- I shouldn't say weren't able. They weren't allowed to travel. Not only did you have to wear the star, but you couldn't leave your place of residence. I don't mean the house itself. But you couldn't go from Budapest to another city or vice versa.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father came down somehow. It was very hard to get on a train because everybody was trying to get back home or wherever they were going, and there was big panic. After all, we were under occupation, even the Hungarians. And so my father and my mother got a note from the doctor that we need to be in Budapest because we were so ill, from the pediatrician. And they had to wear the star.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went to the railroad station. And as I said, this is my experience. This I remember. And we stayed there for a whole day and a whole night. And we were burning with fever, my sister and I. And it was bedlam there. You-- you couldn't move there. Everybody wanted to get on the train. And every time we got on a train, and they saw the star on my parents, they threw us off the train, and I mean literally threw us off the train. And finally, my parents decided that they were going to cover up the star. They were not going to tear it off, but cover it up. And one of them would carry me on that side. And my mother would carry my sister on that side, covering the star. Sure enough, we got on the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think we need to take a quick break.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
